retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An indirect ophthalmoscope is also a handheld device but has a separate source of illumination from a headband and is almost universally performed by an ophthalmologist.  The indirect method provides a wider field of vision for the back of the eye than direct ophthalmoscopy. Pan ophthalmoscopy is a newer form of direct ophthalmoscopy that can be used without pupil dilation while providing a broad view of the fundus.  Slit-lamp ophthalmoscopy utilizes a fixed high-intensity light source and a handheld lens and is typically performed by an ophthalmologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of most retinopathies relies on ophthalmoscopy (also called funduscopy) which can also evaluate the optic disc and the vitreous humor.  Eye dilation is necessary for accurate assessment of any retinopathy.  Ophthalmoscopy comes in few varieties, i.e., direct, indirect, slit-lamp and pantropic.  A direct ophthalmoscope is a small handheld device with its own source of illumination and is typically used most in a primary care setting.  In patients with mild hypertension, direct ophthalmoscopy has been found to have a significant inter- and intra-observer variability. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no complications with non-invasive fundus imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- History and Physical -- Examination technique
retrieved_docs.content: Performed by a retina specialist or pediatric ophthalmologist using the following: Indirect ophthalmoscopy with a 28D or 20D lens RetCam imaging or similar wide-field digital imaging for documentation and telemedicine [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Scanning Laser Ophthalmoscope -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the principle and mechanism of Scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Outline the various non-invasive and invasive dye base imaging techniques performed using a scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Summarize the indications and utility of Scanning laser ophthalmoscope in day-to-day practice. Review the clinical significance of scanning laser ophthalmoscope. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Detachment -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician should complete a slit lamp examination of the anterior segment to look for any abnormalities. A dilated fundoscopic examination should then follow. Significant findings include pigment in the anterior vitreous (Schaffer sign or tobacco dust appearance) or vitreous hemorrhage. An ophthalmologist or optometrist should repeat the fundoscopic examination with indirect ophthalmoscopy with scleral depression so that the entire retina is visualizable up to the ora serrata and the examiner can identify any breaks or tears. [37] [38] Use a B-scan ultrasound to evaluate for detachment if areas of the posterior pole are not visible or if there is an entire view obstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Auxiliary Lenses for Slit-Lamp Examination of the Retina  -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Super 66 : This lens is similar to 78 D.  However, it has 1.0x magnification, simplifying measurements of the optic disc without needing conversion. [11] Thus it is an excellent choice for examining and assessing optic nerve head health. The optical design enhances the stereoscopic view of the central retina to examine the subtle details and changes at the macula and the optic disc. The field of view is up to the equator, and with a dynamic examination, it extends up to the mid-periphery. This lens is a smooth transition for doctors experienced with the 78D, given the similar form factor and optical profile.

retrieved_docs.title: Autorefractors -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-portable- These instruments occupy more space compared to retinoscopy instruments

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Retinopathy -- History and Physical -- Ophthalmoscopic Features
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive retinopathy is primarily diagnosed through characteristic findings observed during ophthalmoscopic examination. These retinal signs reflect the severity and duration of systemic hypertension and help guide clinical assessment and management. These funduscopic features are discussed in detail below.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Eye -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: In ophthalmology, a specialized form of microscopy called slit lamp biomicroscopy is used to visualize anterior and posterior structures of the eye. By using an adjustable slit beam of light, an observer can examine layers of the eye and appreciate depth. For example, an ophthalmologist may evaluate a corneal abrasion in a patient and characterize the severity and extension of the lesion within the layers of the cornea. Several types of illumination techniques exist, including diffuse illumination, direct focal illumination, retroillumination, specular reflection, indirect proximal illumination, and sclerotic scatter. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Night Vision -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The slit lamp is another piece of equipment widely used to evaluate the eye. A slit lamp is a binocular microscope used to examine structures of the eye under high magnification, often used to detect cataracts, a cause of nyctalopia. [8] Additionally, an electroretinogram (ERG) utilizes electrodes placed on the eye's surface to distinguish its response to light flashes. Visual field assessment with kinetic perimetry, using a Humphrey field analyzer or Goldmann perimeter, may be used to determine peripheral vision deficits. Visual field testing with ERG and clinical history are commonly central to diagnosing retinitis pigmentosa, another cause of nyctalopia. The classic triad found under fundoscopic exam for retinitis pigmentosa includes bony spicule pigmentation, optic disc pallor, and vascular narrowing; macular edema and subscapular cataracts are other notable findings. Newer modalities such as adaptive optics scanning laser ophthalmoscopy (AOSLO) have been increasingly utilized for high-resolution retinal examination for earlier detection, treatment, and evaluation. [9] Blood testing of vitamin A (retinol) and glucose levels are other initial evaluations of rod function and retinal vasculature function, respectively. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope -- Indications -- Retroillumination
retrieved_docs.content: In this technique, the microscope is focused on the cornea, while the light is reflected from the iris or the retina. Retroillumination has been further classified as direct or indirect. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Slit-Lamp Biomicroscope -- Indications -- Indirect Retroillumination
retrieved_docs.content: In this method, the observer is at a right angle to the observed pathology or ocular structure. The pathology is seen against a dark, non-illuminated background. The pathologies can be divided based on the optical properties as obstructive, dispersive, or refractile. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Objective Refraction Technique: Retinoscopy -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoscopy may be performed by any personnel trained and found to be proficient in the technique.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Retinopathy -- Evaluation -- Ocular Examination
retrieved_docs.content: On presentation, the patient should be thoroughly tested for: Visual acuity, IOP (intraocular pressure) measurement, Gonioscopy (for neovascularization of iris/angles), Slit-lamp examination (to rule out other ocular manifestations of diabetes mellitus), and Dilated fundus examination with +78 or +90 D lens on slit lamp and with +20 or +28 D lens with an indirect ophthalmoscope - the grading of diabetic retinopathy should be done Additional posterior segment investigations for the evaluation of diabetic retinopathy include:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

In addition, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also manifests in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these diverse findings supports focused clinical assessment and appropriate management of retinal disorders.

Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has its findings localized to the retina. Recognizing that these diverse retinal findings share a common location highlights the importance of comprehensive retinal assessment in the management of diabetic eye disease and other retinal disorders.

Additionally, the retina is noted as the site of the condition described in Arabic as “داء ريفسام.” These connections emphasize the central role of the retina in the manifestation of diabetic retinal disease and other retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of thorough retinal evaluation in patient assessment and management.

Mild, moderate, and general non‑proliferative retinopathy attributed to secondary diabetes mellitus, as well as broader retinopathy and nephropathy linked to the same disease, all arise from the underlying secondary diabetes mellitus and have the retina as the primary finding site. The retina also serves as the anatomical site for retinal detachments, retinal defects, vascular occlusions, and various other forms of retinopathy.
